original_hypothesis.title: uint128 reserve packing may cause unexpected revert/DoS when reserves grow, with inconsistent handling across functions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Reserves are stored as uint128. Some paths explicitly check for overflow (rebalanceSwap checks reserveValidatorToken + amountIn), while mint and executeFeeSwap rely on implicit checked arithmetic when casting/adding to uint128. A pool approaching uint128.max can hit inconsistent revert reasons and become partially unusable (e.g., mint always reverting via panic) even while other operations might still be possible or expected to revert with domain-specific errors.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While hitting uint128.max is unlikely for stablecoins, this is a correctness defect with operational impact: inconsistent overflow handling can break integrations, monitoring, and error recovery logic. If any tokens have very large decimals/supply or the system runs long enough, it can become a real DoS.
deployment_assumptions: A pool can accumulate reserves approaching type(uint128).max due to token decimals/supply or long-term operation
Integrations depend on stable error semantics (custom errors) rather than Panic codes
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol uses uint128 reserves and only rebalanceSwap includes an explicit overflow check; mint/executeFeeSwap do not.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reserves stored as uint128; helper only checks individual amounts, not reserve+amount accumulation

preconditions: Reserve values can grow close to uint128.max in a pool
Users attempt mint/executeFeeSwap/rebalanceSwap near the limit

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: amount bound check without reserve growth bound check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: uint128 reserve additions may revert inconsistently and cause pool operation DoS
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing explicit reserve overflow checks in all reserve-increasing paths

analysis_questions: Are reserve additions in mint/executeFeeSwap protected by any implicit bounds elsewhere?
Should the protocol guarantee reserve magnitudes are far below uint128.max (token choice/decimals)?
Would panic-based reverts break any operational automation?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test reserve growth near uint128.max for all functions
Decide whether uint128 is necessary; consider uint256 or consistent overflow checks
Add explicit custom-error overflow checks for consistency if needed

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a pool’s reserveValidatorToken is near uint128.max, an attacker can grief by inducing attempted mints that revert with Panic and disrupt automated systems expecting custom errors.
prerequisites: Extremely large reserves or unusual token configuration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Mostly operational/availability impact; potential temporary lock of ability to add liquidity or swap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: LPs and swap users of the affected pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: If near uint128.max, recovery may require migrating to a new pool/contract

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests indicated for boundary conditions around uint128 reserve limits

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: high only if attempting to reach uint128.max in production; low for simulation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: panic reverts detectable via traces and error selectors

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
